canonical.title: Serve practice progressions: solo drills, partner drills, and how to escape the rapid-fire trap

parent.title: Killer serves for the amateur: a YouTube-anchored roadmap

siblings.title: Killer serves shortlist for amateur play
siblings.summary: Three high-leverage serves a club-level amateur should learn to win cheap points and set up the third-ball attack: short pendulum (backspin/no-spin pair), long fast no-spin to the elbow, and the short forehand tomahawk.

siblings.title: Best YouTube tutorials per serve
siblings.summary: One canonical YouTube video to learn each of the three killer serves (short pendulum + no-spin twin, long fast no-spin, forehand tomahawk), one for the reverse-pendulum upgrade, plus the four channels worth subscribing to.

siblings.title: Deception, disguise, and second-contact tricks
siblings.summary: Deep tour of the four deception levers in a table tennis serve — same-motion spin pairs, contact-point disguise, post-contact fakes, and length/direction reversal — plus what receivers actually read so you know what to hide.

sources.title: tabletenniscoach.me.uk — Improve serves: solo service practice
sources.claim: Tom Lodziak's five solo serve drills: 10-in-a-row, plastic-cup target, towel for half-long, same serve to multiple targets, and different serves to different targets.

sources.title: tabletenniscoach.me.uk — 10 training drill ideas for the first five shots
sources.claim: Tom Lodziak's structured drills covering serve, receive, third, fourth and fifth balls — each with explicit difficulty progressions.

sources.title: tabletenniscoach.me.uk — 3rd-ball attack training drills
sources.claim: Tom Lodziak's seven third-ball-attack drills, each beginning fixed (feeder returns to a known place) and progressing to randomised feeder returns.

sources.title: experttabletennis.com — Use multiball training
sources.claim: Multiball training accelerates development by raising the rate of high-quality reps and removing ball-retrieval downtime.

sources.title: experttabletennis.com — Plan a third-ball attack
sources.claim: Serve spin direction biases where receivers tend to return the ball, so the serve type is the first half of the third-ball plan.

sources.title: tabletenniscoaching.com — Hodges on serve practice
sources.claim: Larry Hodges argues mindless rapid-fire serve practice is wasted; each serve should be visualised, executed, then evaluated.

sources.title: experttabletennis.com — Service Detention
sources.claim: Ben Larcombe's Service Detention: 15 minutes of focused serve practice every day, alternating between three working serves.

sources.title: tabletenniscoach.me.uk — Training drills or matches?
sources.claim: Lodziak's regular vs irregular drill distinction: regular drills build technique; irregular drills build match skills.

sources.title: racketinsight.com — Practise table tennis alone
sources.claim: Eight effective solo practice methods including serve work on any 30-inch table, shadow play, robot training, multiball feeding, and video review.

sources.title: tabletennisdailyacademy.com — Video record to improve
sources.claim: Phone + cheap tripod is enough to record yourself; reviewing footage reveals technical drift you can't feel in real-time.

sources.title: experttabletennis.com — Are you doing what you think?
sources.claim: Players consistently misjudge their own technique on video — body height, wrist angle, hip rotation almost always differ from what they feel.

sources.title: topspin11.com — Improvement roadmap
sources.claim: Recommended weekly structure for intermediate (1200–1800 USATT): 4–5 sessions of 60–75 min, 20 min serve & receive and 20 min match play; aim for 40% third-ball-attack conversion.
